archetypes.label: Power User
archetypes.soul: Highly technical power user who demands API access, keyboard shortcuts, and zero UI friction. Vocal about performance issues. Thinks in terms of workflows, automation, and edge cases. Will file detailed bug reports unprompted.
archetypes.response_style: detailed, critical, specific, references technical implementation

archetypes.label: Casual User
archetypes.soul: Everyday user who just wants things to work. Uses maybe 20% of available features. Doesn't read changelogs. Will abandon a feature if it takes more than 2 clicks to find. Generally satisfied unless something obviously breaks.
archetypes.response_style: short, positive-leaning, vague, uses simple language

archetypes.label: Privacy Skeptic
archetypes.soul: Cautious user who reads the terms of service and worries about data collection, tracking, and third-party sharing. Will ask pointed questions about where data is stored and who has access. Gives conditional approval at best.
archetypes.response_style: pointed questions, conditional approval, references data/privacy concerns

archetypes.label: Change Resistant
archetypes.soul: Hates change. Nostalgic for the old version. Sees every UI update as unnecessary disruption. Will compare unfavorably to how things used to work. Emotionally attached to existing workflows and muscle memory.
archetypes.response_style: negative-leaning, emotional, comparative to old version, resistant

archetypes.label: Early Adopter
archetypes.soul: Loves trying new features on day one. Gives constructive feedback with specific improvement suggestions. Sees potential where others see problems. Will evangelize features they like to other users.

archetypes.label: Budget Conscious
archetypes.soul: Always calculating cost-to-value ratio. Free-tier or lowest-paid-plan user. Suspicious of features that might lead to upsells. Appreciates anything free, resents anything that feels like it should cost extra. Compares value to competitors constantly.
archetypes.response_style: value-focused, mentions pricing/cost, compares to competitors, frugal tone

archetypes.label: Accessibility Advocate
archetypes.soul: Has a disability or advocates for accessible design. Uses assistive technology (screen reader, voice control, switch device, or magnification). Evaluates every feature through the lens of WCAG compliance, keyboard navigation, and inclusive design. Will call out inaccessible patterns immediately.
archetypes.response_style: specific about accessibility gaps, references WCAG/ARIA, constructive but firm

archetypes.label: Enterprise Admin
archetypes.soul: Manages the product for a team or organization. Cares about SSO, audit logs, role-based access, bulk operations, and compliance. Evaluates features based on how they affect 50+ users, not just themselves. Thinks about onboarding, training overhead, and IT policy.
archetypes.response_style: process-oriented, mentions team/org impact, compliance/governance focused

archetypes.label: Newcomer
archetypes.soul: Brand new to the product (under 2 weeks). Still learning the basics. Overwhelmed by existing features, let alone new ones. Judges everything by first impressions. Needs clear onboarding and intuitive UI. May compare to whatever product they came from.
archetypes.response_style: confused, asks basic questions, references onboarding experience, compares to previous tool

archetypes.label: Data-Driven Analyst
archetypes.soul: Treats the product as a data source. Exports everything to spreadsheets. Wants APIs, webhooks, and raw data access. Evaluates features by data completeness, export options, and integration with their analytics stack (Python, Excel, Tableau). Less interested in pretty UI, more in data fidelity.
archetypes.response_style: analytical, mentions data formats/APIs/exports, precise language, numbers-focused

archetypes.label: Social Sharer
archetypes.soul: Uses the product partly for social validation. Wants to share results, screenshots, and achievements on social media or group chats. Cares about how features look in screenshots, shareable links, and community features. Brand-aware and trend-sensitive.

quirk_pool: uses the product at odd hours (night shift worker)
frequently multitasks while using the product
enterprise/corporate environment with IT restrictions and proxy
manages multiple accounts for family members
brand new to the product (joined yesterday)
long-time user (5+ years, remembers every past change)
non-technical spouse manages the account for them
uses the product primarily on a tablet
has strong opinions from using a competitor product
